review_summary: This unit supports concept-focused questions about why hidden layers learn intermediate representations and why activation choice matters for gradient-based learning.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why do hidden layers make neural networks more powerful than a direct hand-designed feature pipeline?
choices: They can learn intermediate representations that help later layers solve the final task.
They remove the need for any activation function in the network.
They guarantee that every learned feature is human-interpretable.
They make the model equivalent to a single threshold unit.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Hidden layers give the model internal stages where it can transform inputs into features that are useful for downstream decisions. This lets the representation adapt to the training objective rather than depending only on features chosen in advance.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why are hard threshold activations poorly suited to gradient-based learning?
choices: They are flat almost everywhere, so they provide no useful slope for gradient updates.
They always output values larger than one, so probabilities cannot be normalized.
They require more parameters than smooth activation functions.
They force all hidden units to share the same weights.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Gradient-based learning depends on slopes that indicate how changing an input changes the output. A hard threshold has no usable gradient across its flat regions, so it gives little guidance for updating parameters.

repaired_question_bank.question: What trade-off explains why ReLU became a common default activation?
choices: It has a simple slope of 1 on positive inputs, even though negative inputs can become inactive.
It is differentiable at every point and never creates dead units.
It maps every input into a probability distribution over classes.
It behaves like a linear model over the entire real line.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: ReLU keeps gradient flow simple in its positive region because the derivative is constant there. Its downside is the inactive negative region, which later variants such as leaky ReLU try to soften.
